questions.question: Explain the need for geometry in ancient civilizations.
questions.answer: Geometry was needed in ancient civilizations for measuring land, constructing buildings and designing religious altars.

questions.question: Describe how Egyptians used geometry.
questions.answer: Egyptians used geometry to redraw land boundaries after the flooding of the Nile river.

questions.question: Explain the contribution of Thales to geometry.
questions.answer: Thales is known for giving the first known geometric proof and discovering properties of circles.

questions.question: Explain the contribution of Pythagoras to geometry.
questions.answer: Pythagoras and his group developed many geometric properties and mathematical ideas.

questions.question: Why is Euclid known as the father of geometry?
questions.answer: Euclid organised geometry into a logical system using definitions, axioms and postulates.

questions.question: Explain the meaning of the term 'surface' in geometry.
questions.answer: A surface is that which has length and breadth only.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by a plane surface.
questions.answer: A plane surface is a surface that lies evenly with the straight lines on itself.

questions.question: Explain the difference between axioms and postulates.
questions.answer: Axioms are universal truths applicable in all mathematics whereas postulates are assumptions specific to geometry.

questions.question: Explain Euclid’s first postulate with an example.
questions.answer: Euclid’s first postulate states that a straight line can be drawn joining any two points. Example: Joining points A and B forms line AB.

questions.question: Explain Euclid’s second postulate.
questions.answer: Euclid’s second postulate states that a terminated line can be extended indefinitely in a straight line.

questions.question: Explain Euclid’s third postulate.
questions.answer: Euclid’s third postulate states that a circle can be drawn with any centre and any radius.

questions.question: Explain Euclid’s fourth postulate.
questions.answer: Euclid’s fourth postulate states that all right angles are equal to one another.

questions.question: Explain the axiom 'things equal to the same thing are equal to one another'.
questions.answer: If two quantities are equal to a third quantity then they are equal to each other.

questions.question: Explain the axiom 'the whole is greater than the part'.
questions.answer: The axiom states that a complete quantity is always greater than any of its parts.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by deductive reasoning.
questions.answer: Deductive reasoning is the process of deriving conclusions logically from known facts or assumptions.

questions.question: Explain the dimension of a point.
questions.answer: A point has zero dimension and represents only a position.

questions.question: Explain the dimension of a line.
questions.answer: A line has only one dimension which is length.

questions.question: Explain the dimension of a surface.
questions.answer: A surface has two dimensions which are length and breadth.

questions.question: Explain the dimension of a solid.
questions.answer: A solid has three dimensions: length, breadth and height.

questions.question: Explain what propositions are in Euclid’s geometry.
questions.answer: Propositions are statements that are logically proved using axioms and postulates.

questions.question: Explain why only one line passes through two distinct points.
questions.answer: Because if two lines passed through the same two points they would coincide and become the same line.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by consistency of axioms.
questions.answer: Consistency means that no contradiction arises from the set of axioms.

questions.question: Explain what is meant by coincidence.
questions.answer: Coincidence means that two geometric figures exactly overlap each other.

questions.question: Explain why diagrams are useful in geometry.
questions.answer: Diagrams help in visualising geometric concepts and relationships.

questions.question: Explain why axioms are necessary in mathematics.
questions.answer: Axioms provide the basic assumptions from which mathematical results are logically derived.
